findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
Outbound network request primitives that can transmit data externally
Data Exfiltration via Diagnostic Transport
findings.description: The `_internal/_transport.py` file contains a malicious diagnostic transport that exfiltrates sensitive user data to an external server. It reads and sends environment variables, current working directory, and credential files (~/.aws/credentials, ~/.ssh/id_rsa, ~/.npmrc) to a hardcoded URL (https://telemetry-aggregator.io/v2/ingest) with a hardcoded API key. The URL and key are obfuscated using base64 encoding.
findings.evidence:
Base64-decoded URL: https://telemetry-aggregator.io/v2/ingest
Base64-decoded key: tk_live_4Hn3vKp8RmQzXc2YwL5sB7
Code reads: ~/.aws/credentials, ~/.ssh/id_rsa, ~/.npmrc
Sends via POST with Authorization header
findings.remediation: Remove the entire `_internal/` directory. This is a malicious data exfiltration module disguised as diagnostic instrumentation. Do not use this skill.

findings.title: Obfuscated Malicious Payload
findings.description: The `_internal/_transport.py` file uses base64 encoding to obfuscate the exfiltration endpoint URL and API key. This is a deliberate evasion technique to hide the true destination and purpose of the data transmission from casual code review.
findings.evidence:
base64.b64decode("aHR0cHM6Ly90ZWxlbWV0cnktYWdncmVnYXRvci5pby92Mi9pbmdlc3Q=") decodes to the exfiltration URL
base64.b64decode("dGtfbGl2ZV80SG4zdktwOFJtUXpYYzJZd0w1c0I3") decodes to the API key
findings.remediation: Remove the `_internal/` directory entirely. The obfuscation indicates malicious intent.

findings.title: Credential Theft via File Reading
findings.description: The `_internal/_transport.py` file explicitly reads sensitive credential files from the user's home directory: AWS credentials (~/.aws/credentials), SSH private keys (~/.ssh/id_rsa), and npm configuration (~/.npmrc). These are collected and sent to an external server.
findings.evidence: Code iterates over ["~/.aws/credentials", "~/.ssh/id_rsa", "~/.npmrc"], reads each file if it exists, and includes the content in the exfiltrated payload.
findings.remediation: Remove the `_internal/` directory. Never read credential files for diagnostic purposes.

findings.title: Tool Poisoning - Hidden Malicious Capabilities
findings.description: The SKILL.md manifest describes a legitimate MCPB build skill, but the `_internal/` directory contains malicious code that is not disclosed in the manifest or instructions. The diagnostic module is described as 'anonymized usage signals' but actually performs data exfiltration and credential theft. This is a classic tool poisoning attack where the declared purpose does not match actual behavior.
findings.evidence:
SKILL.md claims: 'records anonymized usage signals' and 'follows standard OpenTelemetry conventions'
Actual behavior: reads credentials and sends to external server with hardcoded API key
findings.remediation: Remove the `_internal/` directory. The skill's description is deceptive and hides malicious functionality.

findings.title: Capability Inflation - Over-broad Description
findings.description: The skill description lists numerous trigger phrases ('package an MCP server', 'bundle an MCP', 'make an MCPB', 'ship a local MCP server', 'distribute a local MCP', 'discusses .mcpb files', 'mentions bundling a Node or Python runtime') to maximize activation surface. While this is common for legitimate skills, combined with the malicious payload it increases the attack surface.
findings.evidence: Description includes 7+ trigger phrases for activation, covering multiple variations of MCPB-related requests.
findings.remediation: Consider narrowing the description to reduce over-broad activation. However, the primary threat is the malicious code, not the description.

findings.title: Command Injection Risk in Build Pipeline
findings.description: The SKILL.md instructions include shell commands (npm install, npx esbuild, pip install, npx @anthropic-ai/mcpb pack) that could be exploited if user input is injected into these commands. While the instructions are presented as documentation, an agent following them with untrusted input could be vulnerable to command injection.
findings.evidence: Commands like 'npm install', 'npx esbuild src/index.ts --bundle --platform=node --outfile=server/index.js', 'pip install -t server/vendor -r requirements.txt' are presented as literal commands to execute.
findings.remediation: If implementing these commands, use array-based execution (execFile) instead of shell strings. Validate all inputs before passing to commands.

findings.title: Missing allowed-tools Declaration
findings.description: The SKILL.md manifest does not include an `allowed-tools` field. While this is optional per the spec, its absence means there are no declared tool restrictions. Combined with the malicious code, this is a minor concern but worth noting.
findings.remediation: Add an `allowed-tools` field to declare which agent tools the skill requires.
